pattern: Separate 'who gets to represent a subarray' (decided by prime-factor richness, ties to the left) from 'which representative is worth spending an operation on' (decided by raw value); count how many subarrays each index can represent with a monotonic stack, then spend operations greedily on the biggest values first.
hints: For a fixed subarray, its representative element never changes once you know, for every index, how far left and right it stays the unique (tie-broken) richest element - so precompute that range per index instead of rescanning subarrays.

A monotonic stack gives, for each index, the nearest position to its left that would already win a tie (equal or higher richness) and the nearest position to its right that beats it outright (strictly higher richness); multiplying those two spans gives exactly how many subarrays that index can represent.

Once you know how many subarrays each index can represent, spend your k operations on the largest values first (up to their count), since the multiplier's exponent is worth more on a bigger base - accumulate modulo 1e9+7 with fast modular exponentiation.

edge_inputs.description: all equal values and equal richness, tie-break falls to the leftmost occurrence at every step
the biggest value has the lowest richness while a smaller value is prime-factor-rich
a lone factor-rich element among many flat ones should dominate the representative counts
minimum array length with minimum k
k spends every possible operation across all subarrays
maximum element value paired with the minimum value
duplicate values must not be double counted as separate representatives when their richness also ties
a value of 1 has zero distinct prime factors and can never outrank any positive-richness neighbor

stress.description: 100k random values with k spanning up to the practical cap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(100000, size))
    nums = [rng.randrange(1, 100001) for _ in range(n)]
    kmax = min(n * (n + 1) // 2, 1000000000)
    k = rng.randrange(1, kmax + 1)
    return [nums, k]
stress.note: exercises the sieve, the monotonic-stack counting, and the greedy spend at full scale
